challenges.title: Team Size

steps.explanation: Count employees per department, then keep only groups exceeding 3.

SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3

challenges.title: Label Maker
challenges.description: Using `employees(name, salary)`, return name and a salary label: 'High' if salary >= 70000, 'Medium' if >= 40000, else 'Low'. Output: name, salary_label.
challenges.hint: CASE WHEN lets you create conditional output like an if-else chain.

solution.code:
SELECT name,
  CASE
    WHEN salary >= 70000 THEN 'High'
    WHEN salary >= 40000 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE 'Low'
  END AS salary_label
FROM employees

steps.title: Plan the categories
steps.explanation: Three salary tiers: High for 70000+, Medium for 40000-69999, and Low for everything below. CASE WHEN evaluates conditions top to bottom.

steps.title: Write the CASE expression
steps.explanation: List conditions from highest threshold down, with ELSE as the catch-all.
steps.code:
SELECT name,
  CASE
    WHEN salary >= 70000 THEN 'High'
    WHEN salary >= 40000 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE 'Low'
  END AS salary_label
FROM employees

steps.title: Test edge cases
steps.explanation: An employee at exactly 70000 should get High, at 40000 should get Medium, and below 40000 should get Low.

challenges.title: Multi CTE
challenges.description: Using `orders(customer_id, amount)` and `customers(id, name)`, use two CTEs to find top spenders with names. Output: `name`, `total_spent`. Return only the top 5.
challenges.hint: Define two CTEs: one for totals and one joined with customer names.

solution.code:
WITH totals AS (
  SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
named_totals AS (
  SELECT c.name, t.total_spent
  FROM totals t
  JOIN customers c ON t.customer_id = c.id
)
SELECT name, total_spent
FROM named_totals
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
LIMIT 5

steps.title: First CTE
steps.explanation: Compute per-customer totals.
steps.code:
WITH totals AS (
  SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
)

steps.title: Second CTE
steps.explanation: Join totals with customer names.
steps.code:
named_totals AS (
  SELECT c.name, t.total_spent
  FROM totals t
  JOIN customers c ON t.customer_id = c.id
)

steps.title: Final query
steps.explanation: Select from the second CTE, ordered and limited.
steps.code:
SELECT name, total_spent
FROM named_totals
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
LIMIT 5

challenges.hint: Use GROUP BY category HAVING AVG(price) > 100.

solution.code:
SELECT category, AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category
HAVING AVG(price) > 100

steps.title: Group and aggregate
steps.explanation: GROUP BY category with AVG(price) computes the average per category.
steps.code:
SELECT category, AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category

steps.explanation: HAVING filters after aggregation, keeping only high-average categories.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: All returned categories should have an average price above 100.

challenges.title: Left Anti Join
challenges.description: Using `products(id, name)` and `reviews(product_id)`, return products with no reviews. Output: `id`, `name`.
challenges.hint: LEFT JOIN reviews and filter WHERE reviews.product_id IS NULL.

solution.code:
SELECT p.id, p.name
FROM products p
LEFT JOIN reviews r ON p.id = r.product_id
WHERE r.product_id IS NULL

steps.title: Left join
steps.explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps all products. Products with no reviews will have NULL in the review columns.

FROM products p
LEFT JOIN reviews r ON p.id = r.product_id

steps.title: Anti join filter
steps.explanation: WHERE r.product_id IS NULL keeps only unmatched products.

steps.explanation: Returned products should have zero entries in the reviews table.

challenges.title: Ratio Query
challenges.description: Using `departments(id, name)` and `employees(department_id)`, return each department with its employee count and percentage of total employees. Output: `name`, `emp_count`, `pct_of_total`.
challenges.hint: Use COUNT with GROUP BY and a scalar subquery for total count.

solution.code:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(e.department_id) AS emp_count,
  ROUND(COUNT(e.department_id) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees), 2) AS pct_of_total
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.id, d.name

steps.title: Count per department
steps.explanation: JOIN departments to employees and count per group.
steps.code: COUNT(e.department_id) AS emp_count

steps.title: Calculate percentage
steps.explanation: Divide department count by total employees from a subquery.
steps.code: ROUND(COUNT(e.department_id) * 100.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees), 2) AS pct_of_total

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: All pct_of_total values should sum to approximately 100.

challenges.title: Age Calc
challenges.description: Using `users(name, birth_date)`, return each user with their age in years (as of '2024-01-01'). Output: `name`, `age`.
challenges.hint: Subtract birth year from 2024 using STRFTIME.

solution.code:
SELECT name,
  CAST(STRFTIME('%Y', '2024-01-01') AS INTEGER) - CAST(STRFTIME('%Y', birth_date) AS INTEGER) AS age
FROM users

steps.title: Extract years
steps.explanation: STRFTIME('%Y', date) extracts the year as a string. CAST to integer for arithmetic.
steps.code: CAST(STRFTIME('%Y', '2024-01-01') AS INTEGER) - CAST(STRFTIME('%Y', birth_date) AS INTEGER)

steps.title: Build the query
steps.explanation: Select name and computed age.
steps.code:
SELECT name,
  CAST(STRFTIME('%Y', '2024-01-01') AS INTEGER) - CAST(STRFTIME('%Y', birth_date) AS INTEGER) AS age
FROM users

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Each user's age should be the difference between 2024 and their birth year.

challenges.title: Window Count

challenges.title: Chained CTE
challenges.description: Using `orders(customer_id, amount, order_date)`, use a CTE to find each customer's latest order date and amount. Output: `customer_id`, `last_order_date`, `last_amount`.
challenges.hint: Use ROW_NUMBER in a CTE partitioned by customer, ordered by date descending, then filter for row 1.

solution.code:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT customer_id, order_date AS last_order_date, amount AS last_amount,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders
)
SELECT customer_id, last_order_date, last_amount
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1

steps.title: Rank by date
steps.explanation: ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer gives each order a rank by recency.
steps.code: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn

steps.title: Build the CTE
steps.explanation: Wrap the ranking in a CTE for clarity.
steps.code:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT customer_id, order_date AS last_order_date, amount AS last_amount,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders
)

steps.title: Filter latest
steps.explanation: Select only the most recent order (rn = 1) per customer.
steps.code:
SELECT customer_id, last_order_date, last_amount
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1

challenges.title: Gap Finder
challenges.description: Using `events(event_date)`, return rows where the gap from previous event is more than 1 day. Output: `event_date`, `previous_date`, `gap_days`.
challenges.hint: Use LAG to compare each date with previous row.

solution.code:
WITH dated AS (
  SELECT
    event_date,
    LAG(event_date) OVER (ORDER BY event_date) AS previous_date
  FROM events
)
SELECT
  event_date,
  previous_date,
  event_date - previous_date AS gap_days
FROM dated
WHERE previous_date IS NOT NULL
  AND event_date - previous_date > 1
ORDER BY event_date

challenges.title: Consecutive Streak
challenges.description: Using `logins(user_id, login_date)`, return each user's longest streak of consecutive login days as `longest_streak`.
challenges.hint: Use login_date - row_number grouping trick to identify streaks.

solution.code:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    login_date,
    login_date - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS grp
  FROM logins
), grouped AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    grp,
    COUNT(*) AS streak_len
  FROM ranked
  GROUP BY user_id, grp
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  MAX(streak_len) AS longest_streak
FROM grouped
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id

challenges.title: Rank Race
challenges.description: Using `employees(name, salary)`, return each employee with their salary rank (highest = 1) as `salary_rank`. Output: name, salary, salary_rank.
challenges.hint: RANK() assigns positions with gaps for ties.

solution.code:
SELECT name, salary,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank
FROM employees

steps.title: Understand RANK
steps.explanation: RANK() assigns a position based on ORDER BY. Tied values get the same rank, and the next rank is skipped.

steps.title: Apply the window function
steps.explanation: RANK() OVER with descending salary puts the highest earner at rank 1.
steps.code:
SELECT name, salary,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank
FROM employees

steps.title: Check for ties
steps.explanation: If two employees share salary rank 2, the next rank should be 4, not 3.

challenges.title: Dense Pack

solution.code:
SELECT name, salary,

FROM employees

steps.title: Compare RANK and DENSE_RANK
steps.explanation: RANK skips numbers after ties. DENSE_RANK always uses consecutive numbers. Choose based on whether gaps matter.

steps.title: Apply DENSE_RANK

  DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dense_rank_num
FROM employees

steps.title: Verify consecutive ranks
steps.explanation: If two employees tie at rank 1, the next employee should be rank 2, not rank 3.

challenges.title: CTE Aggregate
challenges.description: Using `orders(customer_id, amount)`, write a CTE to compute each customer's total spending, then return only customers who spent above the average total. Output: customer_id, total_spent.
challenges.hint: A CTE can aggregate first, then the outer query filters using another aggregate.

solution.code:
WITH customer_totals AS (
  SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT customer_id, total_spent
FROM customer_totals
WHERE total_spent > (SELECT AVG(total_spent) FROM customer_totals)

steps.title: Build the aggregation CTE
steps.explanation: First compute each customer's total spending using GROUP BY inside the CTE.

steps.title: Filter against the average
steps.explanation: The outer query compares each customer's total to the average of all totals.
steps.code:
WITH customer_totals AS (
  SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT customer_id, total_spent
FROM customer_totals
WHERE total_spent > (SELECT AVG(total_spent) FROM customer_totals)

steps.title: Confirm the threshold
steps.explanation: Every returned total_spent should exceed the average total spending across all customers.

challenges.title: Pct of Total
challenges.description: Using `orders(department, amount)`, return each order's department, amount, and percentage of the total amount as `pct_of_total` (rounded to 2 decimals). Output: department, amount, pct_of_total.
challenges.hint: Divide each amount by the SUM over the entire table using a window function.

solution.code:
SELECT department, amount,
  ROUND(amount * 100.0 / SUM(amount) OVER (), 2) AS pct_of_total
FROM orders

steps.title: Calculate the grand total
steps.explanation: SUM(amount) OVER () with an empty OVER clause computes the total across all rows, available on every row.

steps.title: Compute the percentage
steps.explanation: Divide each row's amount by the grand total, multiply by 100, and round.
steps.code:
SELECT department, amount,
  ROUND(amount * 100.0 / SUM(amount) OVER (), 2) AS pct_of_total
FROM orders

steps.title: Verify the sum
steps.explanation: All pct_of_total values should approximately sum to 100.

challenges.title: Moving Avg
challenges.description: Using `sales(sale_date, amount)`, return each row with a 3-row moving average as `moving_avg`. Output: sale_date, amount, moving_avg.
challenges.hint: Use AVG with a ROWS BETWEEN frame in the window function.

solution.code:
SELECT sale_date, amount,
  ROUND(AVG(amount) OVER (
    ORDER BY sale_date
    ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ), 2) AS moving_avg
FROM sales

steps.title: Define the window frame
steps.explanation: ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW creates a sliding window of 3 rows: the current row and the two before it.

steps.title: Apply the moving average
steps.explanation: AVG over this window frame produces a rolling 3-row average.
steps.code:
SELECT sale_date, amount,
  ROUND(AVG(amount) OVER (
    ORDER BY sale_date
    ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ), 2) AS moving_avg
FROM sales

steps.title: Handle early rows
steps.explanation: The first row averages only itself. The second row averages two rows. From the third row onward, all three rows contribute.

challenges.title: Top Per Group

  FROM products

WHERE rn <= 2
ORDER BY category, price DESC

steps.explanation: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC) numbers products within each category from most to least expensive.

steps.code:

ORDER BY category, price DESC

challenges.title: First Sale

  ) AS first_order_amount
FROM orders

steps.title: Partition by customer

  FIRST_VALUE(amount) OVER (

FROM orders

challenges.title: Year Over Year
challenges.description: Using `revenue(year, quarter, amount)`, compare each quarter's revenue to the same quarter in the previous year. Output: year, quarter, amount, prev_year_amount.
challenges.hint: Use a CTE and self-join or LAG partitioned by quarter.

solution.code:
WITH yearly AS (
  SELECT year, quarter, amount,
    LAG(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY quarter ORDER BY year) AS prev_year_amount
  FROM revenue
)
SELECT year, quarter, amount, prev_year_amount
FROM yearly
ORDER BY year, quarter

steps.title: Choose the comparison method
steps.explanation: LAG partitioned by quarter and ordered by year gives the same quarter's value from the prior year on each row.

steps.title: Build the CTE with LAG
steps.explanation: Partition by quarter so each quarter tracks its own year-over-year sequence.
steps.code:
WITH yearly AS (
  SELECT year, quarter, amount,
    LAG(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY quarter ORDER BY year) AS prev_year_amount
  FROM revenue
)
SELECT year, quarter, amount, prev_year_amount
FROM yearly
ORDER BY year, quarter

steps.title: Handle the first year
steps.explanation: The earliest year for each quarter will have NULL for prev_year_amount since there is no prior year to compare.

challenges.title: Cumulative Pct
challenges.description: Using `employees(name, salary)`, return each employee with a cumulative percentage of total salary as `cumulative_pct`, ordered by salary descending. Output: name, salary, cumulative_pct.
challenges.hint: Combine SUM OVER with a running frame and divide by the total.

solution.code:
SELECT name, salary,
  ROUND(
    SUM(salary) OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) * 100.0 /
    SUM(salary) OVER (), 2
  ) AS cumulative_pct
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC

steps.title: Compute running and total sums
steps.explanation: SUM(salary) OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) gives a running total. SUM(salary) OVER () gives the grand total.

steps.title: Calculate cumulative percentage
steps.explanation: Divide the running sum by the grand total and multiply by 100.
steps.code:
SELECT name, salary,
  ROUND(
    SUM(salary) OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) * 100.0 /
    SUM(salary) OVER (), 2
  ) AS cumulative_pct
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC

steps.title: Verify progression
steps.explanation: The cumulative_pct should start small and reach 100 at the last row, increasing with each employee.

challenges.title: Pivot Table
challenges.description: Using `orders(product, quarter, amount)`, pivot the data to show each product's total amount per quarter as columns Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4. Output: product, Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4.
challenges.hint: Use SUM with CASE WHEN for each quarter to simulate a pivot.

solution.code:
SELECT product,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q1' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q1,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q2' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q2,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q3' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q3,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q4' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q4
FROM orders
GROUP BY product

steps.title: Plan the pivot approach
steps.explanation: Standard SQL lacks a PIVOT keyword, but SUM with CASE WHEN achieves the same result by conditionally summing for each quarter.

steps.title: Write the pivot query
steps.explanation: Each quarter gets its own SUM/CASE column, and GROUP BY product collapses rows.
steps.code:
SELECT product,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q1' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q1,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q2' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q2,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q3' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q3,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q4' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Q4
FROM orders
GROUP BY product

steps.title: Validate the totals
steps.explanation: For each product, Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 should equal the total amount for that product across all quarters.

challenges.title: Category Rank
challenges.description: Using `products(name, category, price)`, rank products by price within each category (highest = 1). Output: category, name, price, category_rank.
challenges.hint: RANK with PARTITION BY creates independent rankings per group.

solution.code:
SELECT category, name, price,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC) AS category_rank
FROM products
ORDER BY category, category_rank

steps.title: Partition the ranking
steps.explanation: PARTITION BY category resets the ranking for each category. Products compete only within their own category.

steps.title: Apply RANK per category
steps.explanation: Order by price descending so the most expensive product in each category is rank 1.
steps.code:
SELECT category, name, price,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC) AS category_rank
FROM products
ORDER BY category, category_rank

steps.title: Check independence
steps.explanation: Rankings should restart at 1 for each new category. Ties are handled with gaps as expected from RANK.

challenges.title: Nested CTE
challenges.description: Using `orders(customer_id, amount, created_at)`, chain two CTEs: first compute monthly totals per customer, then find each customer's best month. Output: customer_id, best_month, max_monthly_total.
challenges.hint: Multiple CTEs are separated by commas after the WITH keyword.

solution.code:
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT customer_id,
    EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_at) AS month_num,
    SUM(amount) AS monthly_total
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id, EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_at)
), best AS (
  SELECT customer_id, month_num AS best_month, monthly_total AS max_monthly_total,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY monthly_total DESC) AS rn
  FROM monthly
)
SELECT customer_id, best_month, max_monthly_total
FROM best
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY customer_id

steps.title: Build the first CTE
steps.explanation: The monthly CTE aggregates order amounts per customer per month, creating a summary table.

steps.title: Chain the second CTE
steps.explanation: The best CTE ranks each customer's months by total spending and assigns row numbers.
steps.code:
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT customer_id,
    EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_at) AS month_num,
    SUM(amount) AS monthly_total
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id, EXTRACT(MONTH FROM created_at)
), best AS (
  SELECT customer_id, month_num AS best_month, monthly_total AS max_monthly_total,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY monthly_total DESC) AS rn
  FROM monthly
)
SELECT customer_id, best_month, max_monthly_total
FROM best
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY customer_id

steps.title: Verify each customer
steps.explanation: Each customer should appear once, showing the month where they spent the most and the corresponding total.
